messages.content: <tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "oil black smoke signal"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Smoke generation: White smoke use damp grass. Black use oil. 3 bursts = emergency.
Oil makes black smoke. Green leaves white. 3 puffs = rescue needed. 5km.
<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "Raspberry Pi 5 dmesg under-voltage detected hardware check"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Results: Error 0x00050005 indicates supply <4.65V. Hardware checks: USB-C cable gauge (min 24AWG), power supply rating (min 5V/3A continuous), connector integrity, and PCB polyfuse F1 resistance (<0.2Ω normal).

Hardware inspection required:
1. Cable gauge: look for 20-24AWG printed on jacket
2. PSU label: verify 3A continuous not 'peak'
3. USB-C socket: check for debris or looseness

CPU: 63%, RAM: 51%, Temp: 63°C, Battery: 44%, LoRa RSSI: -98dBm, SNR: -2dB
Temp 63°C, Battery 44%, RSSI -98dBm.
<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "hands only CPR compression only"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Hands-only: Continuous compressions 100-120/min, 5-6cm depth, no breaths. Effective for untrained rescuers or when unwilling to give breaths.

Hands-only method:
1. Push 5-6cm deep
2. 100-120 bpm constant
3. No breaths needed
4. Center of chest
5. Until exhausted
<tool_call>{"name": "send_lora_message", "arguments": {"text": "Position 51N 21E status OK"}}</tool_call>

<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "shock management field"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Field: Lay on back on insulation, feet elevated above heart, space blanket, wind protection, check capillary refill and mental status.

Field manage:
1. Back flat
2. Feet up
3. Wind block
4. Check status
<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "Raspberry Pi 5 undervoltage threshold 4.65V vs 4.75V"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Results: Official threshold is 4.65V. If seeing warnings at 4.75V, either measurement error (DMM vs Pi PMIC) or intermittent dips below 4.65V that average to 4.75V. Pi PMIC samples continuously; DMM averages.

Averaging vs instantaneous:
1. Pi sees microsecond dips <4.65V, DMM shows 4.75V average
2. Use oscilloscope to verify actual peaks
3. Trust Pi warning; improve power delivery
<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "dirty bomb iodine exposure survival priorities"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Priority 1: Evacuate if >1 mSv/h. Priority 2: Shelter in dense structure if cannot evacuate. Priority 3: Take KI 130 mg within 4 hours. Priority 4: Decontaminate by removing clothes.

Priority order:
1. Evacuate if >1 mSv/h
2. Dense shelter if trapped
3. KI 130 mg within 4h
4. Decontaminate clothing
<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "active hemorrhage radial artery wound first aid"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Active radial artery hemorrhage: This is high-pressure bleeding. Immediate direct pressure with hemostatic dressing. Elevate hand above heart. If continues, tourniquet on forearm or upper arm 2-3 inches proximal. Do not waste time with pressure points if obviously arterial.

Control active hemorrhage:
1. Immediate pressure with hemostatic dressing
2. Elevate hand well above heart
3. Tourniquet 2-3 inches proximal without delaying for pressure points
<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "arterial bleeding control prehospital tourniquet"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Arterial hemorrhage protocol: Immediate direct pressure with sterile dressing. Elevate limb. If pressure fails, apply commercial or improvised tourniquet 5 cm proximal to wound (not over joints). Tighten until distal pulse disappears and bleeding stops. Note time. Do not loosen.

Stop arterial bleeding now:
1. Compress wound with sterile dressing immediately
2. Elevate limb higher than the heart
3. Apply tourniquet proximal to injury until pulse distal stops

<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "radioactive dispersal device bug out threshold dose"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Bug out if dose rate >500 µSv/h increasing. Shelter if <100 µSv/h and stable. Acute threshold 1000 mSv total. Self-evacuate perpendicular to wind.

1. >500 µSv/h rising = move
2. <100 µSv/h stable = shelter
3. 1000 mSv acute threshold
4. Move cross-wind, upwind

<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "Raspberry Pi 5 undervoltage nothing connected faulty hardware"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Results: If warnings persist with known-good 3A PSU and short thick cable, check: USB-C socket solder joints (cold solder), internal polyfuse resistance increase, or PMIC damage from previous overvoltage. Could be defective unit.

Possible hardware defect:
1. Try different known-good PSU and cable first
2. Check USB-C socket for loose solder (wiggle test)
3. If persists, contact supplier for RMA (rare but possible)

<tool_call>{"name": "send_lora_message", "arguments": {"text": "HQ: Food cache located waypoint 7"}}</tool_call>

<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "Raspberry Pi 5 GPU load voltage drop causes"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Results: GPU adds 400mA+ to CPU load. Total draw peaks 2.8A. Voltage drop = current × resistance. With 0.5Ω cable resistance, drop is 1.4V. Need total chain resistance <0.1Ω for stable 5V at 3A.

Current surge exceeding supply capacity:
1. Calculate drop: V=I×R, reduce R with thick cables
2. Use bench PSU to verify Pi draws <2.5A typical
3. Check if USB devices back-powering

[tool result]
Yes, but carefully. Dissolve 1 heaping teaspoon of 70% calcium hypochlorite in 2 gallons water to make stock solution. Then use 1 part stock to 100 parts water. Dangerous concentration if wrong.

Pool shock method:

2. Add 1 part stock to 100 parts water
3. Wait 30 min
4. Risk of overdose — measure carefully

<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "Raspberry Pi 5 bench power supply undervoltage cable resistance"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Results: Bench supply shows 5V at source but cable adds resistance. With 0.5Ω cable at 2.5A, drop is 1.25V (3.75V at Pi). Also check lead resistance and connector contact resistance. Use thick short wires with good connectors.

Measurement is at supply, not Pi:
1. Measure voltage directly at Pi USB-C pins
2. Use 4-wire Kelvin connection on bench supply
3. Minimize cable length, maximize gauge (18AWG)

<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "SODIS solar disinfection plastic bottle requirements"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Yes. Use clear PET bottles (not PVC). Fill with clear water or pre-filtered turbid water. Expose to direct sunlight for 6 hours (or 2 days if overcast). UV-A radiation inactivates pathogens.

2. Fill with clear water

[tool result]

<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "18650 battery cycle count remaining capacity"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Cycle life varies by chemistry. Standard NMC 18650 cells last 500 cycles to 80% SOH. LFP variants achieve 2000+ cycles. Monitor voltage drop under load to estimate remaining health.
500 cycles to 80% health. Replace when voltage sags under load.

Dress arterial bleed immediately:

2. Elevate limb vertically
3. Windlass tourniquet 2-4 inches above wound if pressure fails

Bow drill: Bow rotates spindle on hearth board using mechanical advantage. Hand drill: Direct hand rotation of spindle on board. Fire plough: Linear rubbing of shaft along groove creates hot char.

<tool_call>{"name": "search_knowledge", "arguments": {"query": "USB-A to USB-C cable Raspberry Pi 5 power undervoltage"}}</tool_call>

[tool result]
Results: USB-A ports max 2.4A (12W) per spec, often 0.9A. USB-A to C cables often resistor-limited to 3A but voltage drop is high. Pi 5 needs 15W+, USB-A sources provide 10W max. Will cause undervoltage under load.

USB-A insufficient power:
1. USB-A limited to 2.4A absolute max (often 0.9A)
2. Pi 5 needs 3A; USB-A cannot deliver
3. Use native USB-C 5V/3A PSU only
